topic: Mobile Pass launch date — eurail-context.md says Sep 2022; primary sources say summer 2020
summary: The shared eurail-context.md baseline states the Mobile Pass launched September 2022. Primary sources (Lonely Planet 2020-09-15, International Railway Journal 2024) confirm summer 2020 in Italy; by 2024 97% of pass purchases are digital.
explanation: The 'Sep 2022' likely refers to a later milestone (e.g., full multi-country rollout or a specific app version), not the original launch. The deck baseline conflated milestones.
synthesis_note: Use 2020 (initial launch, Italy) and note 2024 = 97% digital adoption. Correct the deck framing.

topic: Legal entity name — Eurail B.V. vs Interrail B.V.
summary: Eurail.com About page declares 'Interrail B.V. (until May 1, 2026, Eurail B.V.)'. All public press, news, and breach disclosure as of report date still use 'Eurail B.V.'
explanation: Legal entity rename effective five days before report date; brand transition in flight.
synthesis_note: Refer to 'Interrail B.V. (formerly Eurail B.V.)' on first mention; Eurail B.V. thereafter for continuity with public record.

topic: Mobile Pass launch year — 'Sep 2022' (presentation) vs Sep 21 2020 (primary sources)
summary: The starting brief / Eurail context document cites Mobile Pass as launched 'Sep 2022.' Primary sources (Lonely Planet, IRJ, the Eurail press kit, and the Eurail product page citing 97% adoption) document the launch on September 21, 2020. The 2022 figure may have been a misread of a 2022 press kit, or a launch-rollout-to-Italy date.
explanation: Eurail B.V.'s Mobile Pass was launched September 21, 2020, after a summer 2020 Italy soft-launch. The 2022 date in the brief is incorrect.
synthesis_note: Use 2020-09-21 as the canonical Mobile Pass launch date in all deliverables. Synthesis modules should correct any 'Sep 2022' references inherited from the existing presentation.

topic: Rail Planner App rating: iOS App Store vs Google Play
summary: iOS App Store shows 2.7/5 across 1.5K ratings (US storefront, fetched 2026-05-06); Google Play shows 4.4/5 across 16.4K reviews. The eurail-context.md baseline expected 4.4/5 across ~28k — partially correct (matches Play, refutes iOS).
explanation: Different cohorts: iOS users in the US storefront skew toward older / higher-spend international Eurail buyers (the 50+ bucket-list cohort) who were disproportionately affected by the Jan 2026 breach (passport + payment exposure); Android users skew toward EU-resident Interrail / Gen Z / DiscoverEU. Post-breach degradation is concentrated on the iOS side. App Store also weights recent ratings more heavily.
synthesis_note: iOS 2.7 / Play 4.4 split is a known bimodal pattern with breach-aftermath weighting on the bucket-list cohort. Carried in module 09 (breach-aftermath) and module 15 (scorecard §5 Customer Experience). Treat as duplicate of cf-cust-voice-001.

topic: AppSync vs API Gateway
summary: The eurail-context.md tech-stack hypothesis lists 'AWS AppSync (GraphQL), Lambda, DynamoDB, S3'. Public probing of api.eurail.com returns API Gateway signatures (x-amz-apigw-id), not AppSync. The Android job posting confirms backend uses 'REST and GraphQL APIs' — both exist.
explanation: Eurail likely runs REST endpoints behind API Gateway as the public perimeter and a GraphQL service further inside the network (consumed by mobile apps with credentials). Whether the GraphQL service is AppSync, a self-hosted Apollo server, or commercetools' GraphQL endpoint is unverified from public signals.
synthesis_note: Tech-stack reconstruction has insufficient public evidence to distinguish AppSync vs Apollo vs commercetools GraphQL behind the API Gateway perimeter. Deferred to the technical session per module 20 (Three Asks, Ask 3). Report frames the public stack as REST-via-API-Gateway with internal GraphQL of unverified vendor identity.

topic: AI-in-travel market sizing — broad vs narrow scope
summary: Research and Markets / market.us puts 'AI in Travel' at $165.93B (2025) → $710.57B (2030). MarketsandMarkets / Reports'n'Reports put 'AI in Tourism' at $13.38B by 2030. Two-orders-of-magnitude divergence on scope.
explanation: The $710B figure includes all AI-touched travel commerce (OTA bookings, airline ops, dynamic pricing) — i.e. AI-as-feature-of-travel-spend. The $13B figure is narrowly AI tooling/products sold to travel firms. Both can be true; they measure different things.
synthesis_note: Cite the deck's $165B/$710B figures with explicit caveat that this is the broad scope (AI-touched travel commerce); use the $13B narrow scope when sizing AI-tooling vendor TAM. Do not conflate.

topic: Turkish Airlines MCP server tool count
summary: Existing Eurail presentation deck claims 'Turkish Airlines official MCP server (13 tools)'. Primary source from Turkish Technology states the MCP server hosts 14 different tools.
explanation: The deck's '13 tools' is likely an early launch count; Turkish Airlines added a tool between initial coverage and the current production server.
synthesis_note: Use 14 tools as the current verified count; note in the report that the deck's 13 figure was correct at an earlier moment.

topic: Trainline Trustpilot reception vs claimed 88% AI autonomy
summary: Trainline self-reports 88% of AI Assistant queries handled to completion; Trustpilot/PissedConsumer aggregate is 2.8/5 with refund and human-handoff complaints prominent.
explanation: The 88% measures resolution rate within the AI Assistant flow only — not the long-tail of refund/billing escalations that drive review-platform sentiment. Different denominators.
synthesis_note: Report both: AI is succeeding inside its scope; the painful long tail is outside that scope. Eurail must scope its AI to high-resolution lanes AND fix the human escalation path.

topic: Turkish Airlines MCP tool count — 13 vs 14
summary: The Eurail Context baseline says Turkish Airlines MCP exposes 13 tools. Turkish Technology's own engineering blog (2025-06-04) and AltexSoft survey explicitly state 14 tools.
explanation: Tool count grew between initial launch (13) and current (14). The newer figure is more recent and from the operator itself.
synthesis_note: Use 14 tools — primary source from Turkish Technology supersedes the older Eurail Context baseline.

topic: Conductor Sam self-reported 99.2% accuracy vs absence of independent verification
summary: Conductor Sam claims 99.2% accuracy on a 1,000-question internal test; independent third-party studies of ChatGPT-class travel planners report ~90% of itineraries contain at least one error. The two figures cover different products and methodologies, but the surrogate's number is unaudited and may set a misleading benchmark customers compare Eurail's (non-existent) AI against.
explanation: Different products (Conductor Sam vs general ChatGPT) and different methodologies (self-test vs independent study). Conductor Sam's claim is unverifiable; the third-party 90%-error figure is for general LLMs, not surrogate-curated rail bots.
synthesis_note: The 99.2% figure is unverified vendor self-report; the report does not anchor any claim on it. Module 06 cites the claim with provenance caveat. Modules 16 (federation moat) and 19 (Oraclous-fit roadmap) treat Conductor Sam as a paid-surrogate-exists existence proof and competitive threat, not as an accuracy benchmark.

topic: App Store (iOS) vs Google Play rating divergence on same Rail Planner app
summary: Apple App Store (US) shows 2.7/5 across ~1,500 ratings while Google Play shows 4.4/5 across 16,400 reviews and 1M+ installs — same app, same release cadence. Aggregator mwm.ai shows 3.1/5 across 6.7K worldwide iOS ratings, suggesting US iOS cohort is harsher than worldwide.
explanation: Three drivers likely: (a) iOS US cohort is bucket-list/non-EU buyers facing higher pre-purchase friction, while Google Play global skews EU/young/working-holiday users with lower expectation; (b) Google Play surfaces ratings broadcast vs Apple App Store rating prompt is more selective; (c) Google Play 'install at all' filter selects engaged users with higher tolerance.
synthesis_note: Report all three numbers explicitly: 'Rail Planner is rated 2.7/5 (US iOS, n=1,500), 3.1/5 (worldwide iOS, n=6,700), 4.4/5 (Google Play, n=16,400, 1M+ installs).' Treat the gap as a non-EU buyer experience signal. Recommendation pass should target the App Store gap specifically — that's where bucket-list / Eurail-brand customers live.

topic: Eurail's stated chatbot AI deployment vs visible customer chatbot dissatisfaction
summary: Eurail has deployed Zendesk Ultimate AI internally with reported CSAT 82%, FRT improved 95%, ~50% ticket reduction (per shared eurail-context.md). Visible customer voice on community forum: 'poor quality artificial intelligence, having to deal with it for days trying to get human assistance.' Frontline reported saying 'I don't know how the train system works.'
explanation: Either (a) the internal KPIs measure different things than customer perception (e.g., FRT measures first response, not resolution; CSAT is post-resolution biased); or (b) the published KPIs are aspirational and don't reflect production traffic; or (c) chatbot is good for simple cases (where tickets are reduced) but escalations land in the same delayed queue.
synthesis_note: Treat the Zendesk Ultimate AI CSAT/FRT figures as INTERNAL-METRIC SUCCESS but not external CX validation. The recommendation pass should propose: (a) re-measure post-deployment CSAT using customer-facing review platforms (Trustpilot, App Store post-support reviews); (b) extend AI from internal-tooling layer (where it works) to customer-facing layer (where forum evidence is negative).

topic: Trustpilot Eurail.com vs Interrail.eu — different review counts, possibly different sentiment
summary: Eurail.com Trustpilot has ~6,147 reviews; Interrail.eu has ~6,556 reviews. Interrail.eu reviews show more positive support themes ('great communication, almost immediate replies'), while Eurail.com 1-star reviews concentrate on support delays and reservation pain.
explanation: Eurail.com sells to non-EU buyers (US, AU, CA, JP) with higher product expectation and lower familiarity with rail-pass operator quotas. Interrail.eu sells to EU residents who are more rail-literate and likely younger (gap-year demographic). Same backend support team but different cohort tolerance.
synthesis_note: Segment the customer-voice analysis by brand site: Eurail.com (non-EU, bucket-list, higher expectation) vs Interrail.eu (EU, gap-year, more pragmatic). The non-EU buyer has a worse experience and is the primary target for the AI Journey Assistant recommendation.

topic: Eurail's official Jan 2026 breach statement vs Feb 2026 reversal
summary: Eurail (Jan 15 2026): 'we have no evidence that customer data has been misused or publicly disclosed.' Eurail (Feb 16 2026): 'certain customer data ... offered for sale on the dark web and a sample data set has been published on Telegram.'
explanation: Either (a) Eurail genuinely did not have evidence as of Jan 15 because dark-web monitoring lagged; or (b) Eurail had partial signals but chose conservative public messaging. In either case the reversal is real.
synthesis_note: Report verbatim: the Jan claim was contradicted within 32 days. This sequence is the trust-erosion event, not the breach itself. AI-driven communication recommendation must include verification-grounded statements (no aspirational 'no evidence' framing).

topic: Source-system attribution: presentation cites S3+Zendesk+GitLab as confirmed; Eurail has not validated.
summary: Threat actor claimed exfiltration from AWS S3, Zendesk, and GitLab. Eurail confirmed an unspecified compromise but explicitly has not publicly validated the 1.3TB figure or the Zendesk vector. The existing presentation treats S3+Zendesk+GitLab as confirmed fact.
explanation: The presentation appears to have adopted threat-actor self-disclosure as fact. Eurail's only confirmation is that customer data was exfiltrated and offered for sale; it has not confirmed the storage systems or volume.
synthesis_note: Report S3+Zendesk+GitLab as 'threat-actor-claimed, not Eurail-confirmed' in all subsequent modules. The forensic-disclosure gap is itself the finding.

topic: Trustpilot rating did NOT collapse post-breach despite media narrative.
summary: Trustpilot still shows 3.9/5 in May 2026 across ~6,000 reviews; 'breach trust collapse' assumption is not supported at the aggregate rating level. Recent-window reviews must be tested separately.
explanation: Aggregate Trustpilot scores are slow-moving averages dominated by historical reviews. A 4-month-old breach generates angry recent reviews but does not move a 6,000-review average enough to drop a tier.
synthesis_note: Lifetime-aggregate Trustpilot has a ~0.3-star/year sensitivity ceiling and cannot reflect a 4-month-old acute event regardless of underlying sentiment. Resolved by Wave 2 deep-dive (dd2-trustpilot.jsonl): bimodal distribution (48% 5-star + 16% 1-star tail; n≈6,147) and explicit post-breach 1-star reviews. The report does not use lifetime 3.9/5 as positive signal — see module 09 §5 and module 15 §5.

topic: Stated reason for Trip Planner sunset
summary: Eurail's official announcement gives no reason for the sunset. Two competing interpretations from public evidence: (a) clearing-of-runway for an unannounced AI replacement, (b) cost-cutting / technical-debt retirement amid post-breach budget pressure with no AI successor planned. Both fit the visible signals.
explanation: Public evidence cannot distinguish between the two — the sunset behaviour (no successor named, no roadmap, no data export) is identical under either scenario.
synthesis_note: Both scenarios (a) AI-runway-clearing and (b) post-breach cost cutting fit visible signals; module 10 carries both with branching consequences. Module 20 (Three Asks) surfaces this as a question for Eurail rather than a report-internal claim.

topic: Phocuswright vs Skift on AI travel-research adoption rate
summary: Phocuswright (early 2026) reports 56% of travelers used AI for at least one trip in past 12 months. Phocuswright (2H 2025) reports 39% used AI specifically for travel research/planning. Skift/GHA reports nearly two-thirds of GHA members and ~80% of Gen Z used ChatGPT to plan a trip.
explanation: Different definitions and samples: 'AI for any trip purpose' (broad), 'AI for travel research/planning' (narrower), 'ChatGPT specifically among loyalty-program members' (narrowest cohort + skewed engaged-customer sample). Numbers do not contradict; they measure different things.
synthesis_note: Report all three with explicit framing: ~56% any-purpose AI for travel; ~39% specifically for trip research/planning; loyalty-engaged cohorts and Gen Z run markedly higher (~70-80%).

topic: Baseline gap regression: 'No Is Eurail worth it page' vs current state
summary: The prior audit's baseline gap stated 'No Is Eurail worth it? page'. As of 2026-05-06 the page exists at /en/eurail-passes/everything-you-need-know-about-eurail/is-a-eurail-pass-worth-it (HTTP 200, direct answer in plain HTML, H1 = 'Is a Eurail Pass worth it?').
explanation: The prior audit's claim is no longer accurate. Page was added between the prior-audit date and 2026-05-06. The deeper gap (no schema markup, no last-updated date, hedged answer) persists.
synthesis_note: Update baseline-gap re-verification table to mark this gap as PARTIALLY remediated. The page exists but lacks the schema and freshness signals that win AI citation; treat the AI-citability gap as still open even though the URL gap is closed.

topic: Baseline gap regression: 'FAQ in JS accordions' is partially false
summary: The prior baseline claimed 'FAQ answers inside JavaScript accordions (AI crawlers don't execute JS)'. As of 2026-05-06 the top-level Help Center (/en/help) renders FAQ Q&A as direct HTML. But specialty pages like /en/help/reservations/reservations-eurostar-renfe-tgvinoui still use accordion-style sections, AND the deepest /help/travel-faq tree is still robots-blocked.
explanation: Mixed remediation. Top-level page improved; specialty pages and the canonical FAQ path unchanged. The AI-discoverability outcome is the same on the highest-volume FAQ content because it's robots-blocked anyway.
synthesis_note: Mark this gap as MIXED in the re-verification table. The accordion problem is now secondary; the robots-block on the FAQ tree is the dominant remaining gap.

topic: Refreshed score (38) vs prior baseline (65) — methodology change
summary: Refreshed score 38/100 vs baseline 65/100. The 27-point delta partially reflects new gaps (breach-response block, Wikipedia staleness) that the prior rubric likely did not weight, not solely strategic regression.
explanation: Different rubric weights. Prior rubric appears not to have included breach-aftermath, third-party-surrogate, or Wikipedia-entity-health dimensions because the breach hadn't happened.
synthesis_note: Report both numbers with explicit methodology disclosure. Headline framing: 'GEO surface has regressed because the breach exposed new gaps the prior rubric didn't measure'.

summary: Eurail/Interrail's own About-Us pages (eurail.com and interrail.eu, ev-rebrand-001 and ev-rebrand-002) declare the legal entity is now 'Interrail B.V.' as of May 1, 2026, while every external aggregator (Wikipedia ev-rebrand-014, Tracxn, Creditsafe, LeadIQ, Northdata) continues to list 'Eurail B.V.' as of May 6, 2026.
resolution: RESOLVED — Eurail's own statement is authoritative; aggregators lag because the rename took effect 5 days before the report date and registries refresh on cycles measured in weeks. Note: AI search engines (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude) sourced from these aggregators will return outdated entity names for the next 4–8 weeks; this is itself an AEO action item.

summary: Press-release boilerplate on interrail.eu shifted between April 9, 2026 ('Eurail B.V. is owned by over 35 European railway and ferry companies', ev-rebrand-003) and May 4, 2026 ('Interrail B.V. is owned by over 35 European railway and ferry companies', ev-rebrand-004) — confirming a hard cutover at May 1.
resolution: NOT A CONFLICT — sequential evidence brackets the rename. Logged as a conflict-record only because two contemporaneous Eurail press releases name the same entity differently; the dating resolves it.

topic: Eurail Trustpilot aggregate score (3.9/5) appears unaffected by Jan 2026 breach despite acute customer trust event
summary: Wave 1 flagged that Eurail's Trustpilot stays at 3.9/5 despite (a) Jan 14 2026 breach disclosure, (b) Feb 16 2026 dark-web sale confirmation, (c) Mar 27 2026 individual customer notification (91-day delay), (d) class-action filings, (e) verbatim breach grievances on the platform itself. How is this possible?
explanation: Three-part resolution: (1) MATHEMATICAL CEILING — lifetime aggregation over 6,147 reviews has a ~0.3★/year sensitivity ceiling. Even if every Jan-Apr 2026 review were 1★ (estimated ~400-600 reviews), the lifetime mean would only fall to ~3.6-3.7. Trustpilot CANNOT mechanically collapse a multi-year base in 4 months. (2) DISTRIBUTION HIDES THE TAIL — the 3.9/5 mean masks a bimodal distribution: 5★ 48%, 1★ 16%. Breach-driven 1★ reviews stack into the existing 16% bucket, raising it but not visibly. (3) NEW THEMES, OLD STARS — pre-breach 1★ reviews already concentrated on app/reservation/disruption pain; post-breach 1★ reviews ADD security themes on top. The total 1★ percentage moves slightly, but the qualitative composition has shifted dramatically.
synthesis_note: Do NOT cite '3.9/5 Trustpilot rating' as evidence the breach has not damaged trust. The aggregate is high-inertia and breach-blind by construction. Instead, cite: (a) the 16% 1★ tail (n≈984 reviews) as the addressable AI-strategy population; (b) post-breach review themes (passport leak, no compensation, phishing fear) as net-new grievances; (c) the Eurail.com (3.9) vs Interrail.eu (4.1) gap as the cohort-routing problem an AI personalization layer would address. The 'aggregate has held' framing in the existing presentation must be qualified — the aggregate is a lagging indicator, not absence of harm.
